futures. These are the type of trades
I'm after. Big trades, not pennies. Now,
I know that might sound wild to you, but
the indicator that I'm about to share
with you is designed to take advantage

why I'm able to spot the best riskreward

trading live with us every day in our
trading community. new traders, busy
traders, people from all walks of life
using the same strategy. And the best
part, it's stupid simple and you
probably already have it in your charts.
However, most people use that indicator
the wrong way. And like with most
indicators, if you use them the wrong
way, they're going to cause more damage
to your account than good. And
unfortunately, I had to learn that the
hard way. When I first started trading
12 years ago, I thought that all I need
is one indicator that can predict the
future. I blew up account after account
until I finally realized the truth. Most
indicators are lagging. No indicator can
predict the future. But the one that I'm
about to share with you shows with
clarity when the price is too expensive
to buy or too cheap to sell. And that
indicator is called the RSI. The RSI
don't lie. So, in this video, I'm going
to walk you through my exact three-step
method. And at the end, I will show you

to squeeze even more out of this
indicator for more accuracy and more
profitability. Let's get started. So,
before we start, we want to make sure
that we set up our charts the right way.
For that example, I use here the
platform Trading View. So, the first

View, all you need to do is look here at

do is doubleclick and add it to your
charts. and we're going to leave the
settings as they are the standard way.
Now the second thing we want to do is we
want to set up our charts to one hour.
You see right here next to indicators
you have the time frame. The 1 hour is
important because the RSI gives us the
best and cleanest signals and
opportunities on the 1 hour chart. Once
you choose the one hour, you're ready to
go. So what exactly is the RSI and what
information it is showing us? The RSI
measures how fast the price is moving
over time. When the price moves too
fast, too far in a short period of time,
usually this is an emotional move. So
that indicator picks up when a price
goes off the rails and starts moving too
fast either to the upside or to the
downside. and we're going to use that
information about this emotional move in
our advantage. So this is how the RSI
looks like on our charts. The lower part
of the RSI, this zone right here is RSI
30. And that means that the price is
oversold. The upper part of the RSI in
this case 70 means that we are in
overbought levels. So for now I just
want you to remember that RSI lower than
30 means oversold and RSI higher than 70
means overbought. Now if you don't
understand all of it, don't worry about
that. We're going to go through plenty
of examples and through the exact rules
and I'm sure you will understand
everything in depth by the end of this
video. So now that we know that, let's
get into our rules and into step number
one. And by the way, the rules are
extremely simple. When the RSI is
oversold at 30 or below, we buy. When
the RSI is overbought at 70 or above, we
sell. And if it's anywhere in between,
we do nothing. No guesswork. Now, let's
take a look at our first example. So,
this right here is the stock PLTR and
the 1 hour chart. Let's take a look at
the RSI. As we can see, the RSI is
oversold below 30 based on the
information that I gave you. What do we
do in this example? Exactly right. We
buy. Now, let's go ahead and replay and
see what happens. The price goes
straight towards the overbought area.
Now, let's take a look at another
example. This right here is the NASDAQ
ENQ futures and the same time frame 1
hour time frame and as we can see we are
right at the 70 RSI based on the
information that I gave you. What would
you do here if you had to place a trade

look what happens. As we can see the
price dropped straight into the oversold
area. Here is another example. Same
asset, same time frame. So in this
example, RSI is way below oversold and
therefore we're going to place a buy
order. And as we can see, the price
shoots straight from oversold towards
the midpoint of the RSI. Now, you can go
a pretty long way just by following this

I told you that by adding one more
filter, you can boost your accuracy by
20 more percent. Which leads me to step

we want to check on the chart if the RSI
is oversold, meaning 30 or below. We
want to make sure that we are at the
very important support zone, meaning at
the previous low or a previous breakout
zone. If the RSI is 70 or more, meaning
overbought, the same thing. We want to
zoom out and we want to make sure that
this price is right at the previous
resistance zone, previous high or
breakdown zone. Now, I know that might
sound confusing to you if you're new,
but don't worry. We're going to look
through plenty of examples. What we're
doing here is just we're adding more
accuracy to our trades. So why do we
look at these support and resistance
areas? It's actually very simple. It's
because this is where smart money puts
their orders. So when the RSI is
oversold and it's dropping into a
support area, this is where big
institutional traders are placing their
buy orders and this is where retail
traders are placing their stop- losses.
So all of that acts as a pocket of
liquidity and this is where the price
most likely reverses and it works the
same way in reverse. If the RSI is
overbought right into resistance, this
is where big institutional money are
placing their sell orders and small
retail traders like you and I are
placing their stop- losses. And
institutional traders know that this is
what creates the pocket of liquidity for
them to sell into. And this is what we
are going to use in our advantage. And
these areas act as magnets which makes
our RSI signals more reliable and adds
more accuracy. So let me show you how
that exactly looks like. So this right
here is the NASDAQ futures. Same time
frame 1 hour chart. We want to stick
with the higher time frames. And as we
can see here, we're way below 30 RSI.
We're pretty oversold here. So in our
step number two, we want to zoom out and
see if there is a previous support area
where potentially big money is going to
start placing their buy orders. So for
that, I'm going to zoom out here. And as
we can see, this was a previous low and
it was tested two times. This makes this
zone a pretty solid support area for our
trade. So now we know that big money
will potentially reverse the price at
this area. And this is where most retail
traders like you and I are placing their
stops. And I'm going to be honest with

would look at the chart like that and I
would think this is totally bearish. It

I would start shorting into that. And I
would be totally shocked when the price
reverses and starts to go against me
because I would not use this information
with the oversold zones and the
important liquidity zones. So now we can
place a buy order because we have our
two criterias filled. We are right at
this oversold area below 30 and we are
at a very important support zone. As we

towards the previous breakdown zone and
resistance. And if you entered around

takerit area. And by the way, this is
the exact trade I took live in our

70 overbought. And now we want to zoom

money is going to place sell orders. I'm

is a breakdown area. So, this makes our
cell that we're going to place a very
nice risk versus reward setup. And
because we have the two criterias
filled, we're right at overbought and
we're at a very important resistance
zone, right at the high and breakdown
zone. I'm going to place a short sell
order. And as we can see the price drops

and support zone and goes also into this

versus reward ratio. This means if you

how you can capture this big risk versus

trades because you're at an extremes
overbought or oversold RSI and you're at
a very important liquidity zones where
institutional monies is going to reverse
the price and you can just take
advantage of that information. Now, that
alone is going to sort out the noise and
provide you with this huge setups and
trades. But I want to add one more
filter that can give you even better
trades, better accuracy, and more
clarity where to enter. So, for that,
we're going to use a very specific
candlestick formation, which leads me to
our step number three, using the
pitchfork candlestick formation for our
entries. So let's summarize first. In
our step number one, we were looking for
overbought and oversold RSI zones.
Meaning if the RSI is below or at 30, it
is oversold and we're looking to buy. If
the RSI is at 70 or above, we're looking
to sell or short sell. In our step
number two, we added one more very
important filter. We're looking not only
that a certain area is overbought or
oversold, but we're also now zooming out
and making sure that when we look to the
left, if the asset is oversold, we are
at a very important support zone or if
the asset is overbought, we're at a very
important resistance zone. And now we
want to add one more thing in our step
number three, which is the pitchfork
candlestick formation. So what exactly
is the pitchfork? When we are at
support, we want to look for three
candlesticks. The first candlestick
right at support is when the price goes
down. Normally the candlestick is red.
When the price stops right at support
and it's oversold, we want to look for
one first green candlestick. And the
entry happens as soon as the price
starts to go above the high of that
first green candlestick. So the entry in
this case happens on the third
candlestick. If we're short selling is
the other way around. We're looking at
overbought zones 70 plus RSI and we're
looking that this is happening at
resistance. And normally we will have a
few green candlesticks in a row right
into resistance. And we want to see one
red candlestick first closing red and
the price going below that first red
candlestick. So in this case, our entry
is below that low on the third
candlestick. Now let's take a look how
that looks like in real life because
this is the exact trade I took live in
our trading community. And that right
here is the ES one hour chart. Remember,
we're sticking with that time frame. And
as we can see, we're right here at the

be our entry and the stop-loss above.
Now, just to demonstrate you how that

And based on that nice risk versus
